data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Blue Owl Technology Finance Starts Trading
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up–highlighting individual news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the second week of June.

Market Action

BDCs were down slightly on the week, in line with equity markets, even as fixed-income markets rallied on lower than expected inflation. Month-to-date, the sector is fairly flat overall.

BDC valuations remain slightly below their historical average, in aggregate.

Market Themes

The Blue Owl Technology Finance Corp (OTF) started trading. As expected, it had a tough first few days of trading, falling 6.4%. The reason is the initial lock-up waiver on 5% of shareholder positions. In the context of a small float of just 5% of shares outstanding, that was a sizable amount of selling pressure.

The company is focused on the Technology sector in the upper middle-market segment. The portfolio yield is 10.6% and leverage was low at 0.5x - likely in anticipation of the lock-up expiry. The first will come towards the end of this year and two more in the first half of next year.

Net investment income has fallen over the past year, in line with the broader sector. NII yield works out to 10.35% per share at the current price, with the discount at around 6%.

It’s fairly attractive now, but it will likely go through a similar volatile trading period as the other recently listed IPOs. In particular, the first lock-up expiry on 9-Dec should see a sizable drop in the stock. Over the past year, M&A has been fairly slow - the common equity sleeve of OTF should deliver value once deal activity takes off. Another Blue Owl BDC OBDC remains in the Income Portfolios and reflects the ability of the manager to deliver value.

OTF is right on the fair-value line on a 3-year horizon, given its below-average total NAV return matches its below-average valuation. On a 5-year timeline, it looks slightly cheap. We would look to add the stock at a double-digit discount.

Market Commentary

Capital Southwest (CSWC) announced a switch from a quarterly to monthly payout. The $0.58 quarterly dividend was rounded up to $0.1934 per month. CSWC joins the ranks of HRZN, MAIN and PSEC and other BDC that also pay monthly dividends.

There are three misconceptions having to do with this move. The first is that the monthly dividends are so attractive to retail investors versus quarterly dividends that they should drive the stock’s premium higher - after all just look at where GLAD and MAIN trade.

The first-order reason for the very high premiums of GLAD and MAIN is their high total NAV returns - the two companies are 2 of the top 3 performers in our coverage. Unlike CSWC, at-the-market issuance is not the driver of this outperformance.

Outside of these two outperformers, we also have PSEC and HRZN, which pay monthly as well and are near the bottom of the list in our coverage. Is there any evidence that BDC valuations benefit from moving to a monthly dividend? HRZN is very expensive on our fair-value chart however the other three BDCs are near fair-value, indicating little, if any impact, of the dividend frequency on valuation.

The second misconception is that investors benefit because the new monthly dividend is slightly more than 3x the previous quarterly dividend, meaning the company, in effect, increased its annual dividend. However, the dividend in common shares is not what drives wealth - the underlying income of the company is what drives wealth and its overall return. The dividend is just an arbitrary payment from the company to common shareholders. In short, the increase in the dividend is not a wealth-increasing measure for investors - the underlying income, which is unchanged, is the real driver of wealth.

The last misconception is that there is a compounding effect of the monthly dividends over quarterly ones. However, given what drives wealth for BDC common shareholders is not the dividend but the underlying income of the company, the frequency of the dividend does not matter from a return perspective. In short, common share dividends don't compound - what compounds are liability dividends (i.e. bond coupons). Common share dividend size or frequency don't change the overall return because the company’s assets aren’t affected by dividend frequency and if the company's assets aren't affected, neither is its income, which, again, is what drives common shareholder returns.

Prospect Capital (PSEC) announced that it adjourned its special meeting of stockholders to 17-June to allow more time to solicit votes to renew the company’s authorization to sell shares of its common stock below the company’s NAV. Asking for this is fairly common for BDCs, though few actually act on it and issue stock below the NAV. Doing this would be highly dilutive to PSEC’s NAV, given the enormous discount of the stock.

It’s no surprise it didn’t have much luck in the first meeting. This doesn’t mean the stock can’t grow its common share count - it has been doing this just fine via the dividend reinvestment program, where investors get a 5% discount on the price by getting their dividend in new shares. It also solved the problem of new equity another way - by relying on preferred shares rather than common shares to raise equity. Ultimately, the company is getting a good amount of new equity; it’s what it’s doing with it which is the problem.

data.title: 3 Charts I Am Thinking About
data.description:
Here are some charts I think I am thinking about.

1) Too Late, Jerome?

Wednesday’s Fed meeting was mostly uneventful. They downgraded their economic outlook marginally and raised inflation expectations slightly. There was an immediate response of “stagflation is coming”, which I still find silly. The Fed projects 1.4% economic growth and 3% inflation.

I’ve been saying this for a long time now, but this is not stagflation. Stagflation is stagnant growth and high inflation. Nominal GDP is expected to be about 4-5% in this scenario. And PCE inflation has averaged 3.3% since 1960.

We do not have high inflation any more. And we don’t even have stagnant growth. We have very average inflation right now. Perhaps a bit on the low side. And we have low GDP, not stagnant GDP. So no, this still isn’t a stagflationary environment, but the Fed seems overly concerned about something resembling that. And because of this, it looks like they’re starting to fall behind the curve a bit here.

One person who clearly agrees with this view is Donald Trump, who didn’t think the Fed decision was at all uneventful. He took to his social media platform to blast Powell:

“Too Late—Powell is the WORST. A real dummy, who’s costing America $Billions! ‘Too Late’ Jerome Powell is costing our Country Hundreds of Billions of Dollars. He is truly one of the dumbest, and most destructive, people in Government, and the Fed Board is complicit. Europe has had 10 cuts, we have had none. We should be 2.5 Points lower, and save $BILLIONS on all of Biden’s Short Term Debt. We have LOW inflation! TOO LATE’s an American Disgrace!”

Not nice! But I don’t think he’s totally wrong here. There’s a very reasonable argument that the Fed should be loosening here. We have benign inflation and there are enough cracks in the labor market that you could be loosening policy slowly.

But Trump didn’t do himself any favors with all this Tariff uncertainty, and that’s ultimately what the Fed is worried about now. The last thing they want to see is a material flow-through from tariff price hikes. I don’t think that’s a significant risk, but I can see the argument there, so while I lean towards cutting, I can also see the rationale for sitting tight.

In any case, I am not sure how much any of this matters at this point. I’ve long been on the record saying that Trump will appoint a yes-man in 2026 and the job interview for the next Fed Chair is basically going to come down to whether that person will advocate strongly for cuts. So you have to think the probability of the Fed catching up with the ECB will rise as we get into 2026. That’s especially true if the labor market continues to soften.

2) Nothing Ever Happens

I spoke with Joseph Adinolfi of MarketWatch earlier this week about a meme that has been going around market circles lately – this idea that “nothing ever happens”. This is essentially another way of saying “stocks only go up”. That is, even though it seems like lots of stuff happens in the economy, nothing is really happening that the markets care about too much.

In just the last few years, we’ve had the big Covid scare, the bank panic, the tariff scare and now the WW3 scare with Iran. But if you’d slept through it all and looked at stock prices, you might think that nothing actually happened as markets have largely shrugged this off.

And that’s all very true at a macro level, but it’s not quite so true at a micro level. The way I would describe most of this is that the financial markets and the economy are primarily dictated by huge secular trends. Those secular trends sometimes get knocked off course temporarily, but those secular trends generally reassert themselves.

For example, the biggest macro trend of them all is that human beings like to make progress. Every single day, there are billions of humans waking up trying to solve problems and push forward through life. Betting against this is trying to swim against a raging river current. Good luck betting against human progress in the long run. This is the most powerful secular trend that pushes stock prices up over the long term.

That said, it doesn’t mean nothing ever happens. In fact, one of the reasons I love financial markets is because something happens every day. And even though most of it doesn’t impact our portfolios in a manner that requires big shifts, it is still interesting in the micro sense.

For example, in the above article I am cited saying that a whole heckuva lot has happened in forex markets this year. In fact, I’d argue that’s been the epicenter of the biggest trades this year. The US Dollar Index is down 7%, which is a pretty big move in just 6 months.

But what’s more interesting about this is the knock-on effect to other instruments. For example, foreign stocks are up 14.5% this year, while US stocks are up just 1.6%. So yeah, it might look like the tariffs did nothing, but in reality they helped contribute to a double-digit outperformance of foreign stocks vs domestic stocks because the tariff policies have directly hurt the USD. So, even though it might look like nothing has happened on an absolute basis, a lot has happened on a relative basis.

So, I wouldn’t say nothing has happened this year. In fact, I’d say 2025 has been one of the more eventful and potentially regime changing years we’ve seen in a long time.

3) Will “Nothing Happen in the Future?”

The “nothing happens” narrative is interesting because it strikes me as something that’s indicative of the times. We have been in a stock market environment where everyone just assumes stocks only go up. And yes, that’s mostly true.

But it’s not the direction of stocks that typically concerns me, but the path they take to getting there. There’s been a lot of chatter in recent years about high valuations and future returns. We know that current valuations are a poor predictor of short-term returns, but are typically a good predictor of future 10-year returns.

But again, it’s not the direction of the returns that concerns me. If we think of this within my Defined Duration model, then global stocks are about an 18-year instrument at present. US stocks are an even longer instrument at about 23 years if we assume lower expected returns.

But over most rolling 10-year time horizons, the stock market will pretty reliably generate positive returns. How positive? Well, we don’t know. But again, the directionality over 10-year time horizons is highly probable to be positive over that long of a time horizon.

But the path to getting those positive returns varies significantly. And high valuations are very consistent with worse future risk-adjusted returns. In other words, you might not get lower returns, but you will very likely get bumpier returns, which means more uncertainty of returns.

I love the 10-year time horizon because it’s pretty close to what an aggregate stock/bond index is. It’s close to the total market portfolio, so it’s the time horizon that the average investor holds in a diversified portfolio (if global stocks are an 18-year instrument and bonds are a 6-year instrument, then a 50/50 portfolio is about 11.5 years).

And that makes it the most important portfolio in many ways. But it’s also the most confusing portfolio for us to match to our liabilities to because it’s the inbetweener portfolio. It’s not short enough like cash where we can definitively match a cash asset with a short-term liability like T-bills.

And it’s not long enough where we can just match a long duration instrument to it and ignore it like we can with stocks (anyone with a 20-year time horizon, e.g., can pretty reliably invest in stocks and just ignore them for a long time).

That 10-year time horizon is a sort of financial planning no man’s land. Which is why the path to generating stable returns over this period is so important – it’s the portfolio that is most behaviorally challenging in my experience.

And what does the data say right now? It says that current valuations are highly consistent with low risk-adjusted returns and since stocks are the main contributor to 10-year volatility in the global market portfolio it makes this an especially tricky time horizon for financial planning needs.

And that’s important because it means that even if we can expect positive future returns over the coming 10 years, the path of those returns is likely to be highly uneven because the expectations for high returns and “nothing to happen” is high.

And that’s why diversification is more important than ever for anyone who has a multi-temporal set of financial planning needs. In other words, I expect a lot to happen in the coming 10 years. And much of it will be surprising, which is why stock returns will be more volatile than usual.

I hope you have a great weekend and as always, stay disciplined out there!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Service Corporation International: A Stock To Take To The Grave
data.description:
Forming emotional attachments regarding companies is not a good idea when it comes to investing. But if you have been involved in the investment game long enough, I think it might be inevitable. One company that I am particularly fond of, as morbid as this might sound, is funeral home and cemetery owner and operator Service Corporation International (NYSE:SCI). It was one of the first five companies that I ever bought shares of, and I remember fondly working on a 40-page research report on it in graduate school. Even though I do have positive feelings about the company, I have always done my best to remain as impartial as possible. Fortunately, my calls on the firm have been right over time.

Since I last wrote about the company in January of this year, in an article in which I reaffirmed the business as a ‘buy’ candidate, the stock is up only 3.8% compared to the 4.3% rise that the S&P 500 has enjoyed. But since I originally rated it a ‘buy’ over nine years ago in April 2016, the stock is up an astounding 248.4%. That comfortably outpaces the 188% rise enjoyed by the S&P 500 over the same window of time. Even though the company experienced some volatility because of the COVID-19 pandemic, the overall trajectory for the firm has generally been higher. After all, as the population of the US continues to grow, the number of deaths taking place also increases. Long term, this should continue to be the case. And while shares of the company are not exactly cheap, they do seem attractively priced for a high-quality industry leader. Because of this, I have no choice but to keep the company rated a ‘buy’ for now.

A play on your imminent demise

When it comes to the North American funeral home industry, Service Corporation International is the giant in the space. As of the end of the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year, it boasted 1,985 locations in operation. 1,328 of these were funeral homes located in the US. The firm has another 161 in Canada. It also has 481 cemeteries in the US and 15 located in our neighbor up north. Long ago, it used to have operations in other nations as well. France was one example. But because of the regulatory environment in those nations, and an overly aggressive expansion strategy, the firm ended up having to sell off all of its foreign facilities with the exception of those based out of Canada.

The entire strategy of the company revolves around the idea of asset clustering. When you have similar assets in close proximity to one another, there is the opportunity to benefit from using assets that might be dormant for one location for another location that might need it. This has allowed significant cost savings. And even though this is the largest player in the space, it still has plenty of opportunity to grow. I already covered this in my previous article. But in 2024, the firm accounted for just 17% of industry revenues in the markets in which it operated. All other major players in the space combined accounted for about 9%. That leaves another 74% that is controlled by independent operators. These are often small shops, many times just a single location. This fragmented market gives the company the opportunity to grow rather easily by means of acquisition and to capture additional cost savings from integrating new assets into its network.

Overtime, management has done a fine job of growing the business. Take, as an example, the most recent quarter, which would be the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Overall revenue hit $1.07 billion. That was up modestly from the $1.05 billion the business reported one year earlier. This rise occurred even though cemetery revenue for the company actually contracted from $440.7 million to $432.9 million. Management claimed that this was because of a reduction in pre-need property revenue caused by reduced pre-need property sales. It's difficult to tell why that ended up occurring. However, management mentioned in their first-quarter earnings call that this was likely a timing issue. In fact, they said that for April, these sales looked ‘very good’.

The growth for the company occurred within its Funeral segment. Revenue managed to rise from $604.7 million to $639.5 million. A good chunk of this, about $13.4 million, stemmed from acquisitions and new construction activities. However, comparable funeral revenue still managed to rise by 3.9%. This was in spite of the fact that pre-need funeral sales production actually dropped a whopping $32 million year over year, with some of that because of the firm's transition to its new pre-need insurance provider that occurred in the second half of the 2024 fiscal year. Non-funeral home pre-need sales production dropped about $20 million year over year because of the firm’s decision from the sale of trust to insurance-funded pre-need contracts.

This has been an ongoing process, though the firm has finally achieved about 80% of production coming from these contracts. So that process seems to be largely complete. Long term, this is a transition that will pay off. For those not familiar with it, because of regulatory issues, such as a big settlement that the firm paid out last year in California, and also because of business model considerations, the company has been moving from a trust-funded model to an insurance-funded model for its pre-need contracts. Under the trust-funded model, customers would have to pay money upfront that would then be placed into a trust. Those funds would be invested and then paid out when services were ultimately rendered. However, under the insurance model, the company sells underwritten life insurance policies in order to fund pre-need contracts. This should make contracts more affordable for customers. Simultaneous to this, the firm will be able to benefit from insurance premiums and commissions on an upfront basis. If everything goes according to plan, management expects a return to single-digit sales growth in 2026 and improved profit margins associated with these contracts.

With revenue rising, profitability for the company also continued to grow. Year over year, net income for the company grew from $131.5 million to $142.9 million. Adjusted net profits also expanded, rising from $131.9 million to $139.6 million. Operating cash flow jumped nicely from $220.1 million to $311.1 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a smaller increase from $220.5 million to $229.6 million. Lastly, EBITDA for the business expanded from $309.1 million to $327.3 million. In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2023 and 2024 as well. While some cash flow figures did improve year over year and while revenue increased also, that chart shows that 2024 was a much more mixed year than what we have seen recently.

For 2025, management has mentioned that adjusted earnings per share should be between $3.70 and $4. At the midpoint, we would be looking at adjusted net profits of around $559.4 million. Adjusted operating cash flow will probably be around $860 million, while EBITDA should be somewhere around $1.20 billion. Using these estimates, and also using the historical results from 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are multiples that, typically, would put the company in the fair value range as far as I am concerned. But considering the long-term outlook for the industry, its overall stability, and the fact that it is the market leader, makes me feel a bit more confident with a bullish assessment. There aren't very many publicly traded companies in this space. But in the table below, I did compare it to two other similar firms. On both a price-to-earnings basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, it ended up being in the middle of the group. And on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, it is the cheapest of the three.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow

EV / EBITDA
Service Corporation International 20.0 13.0 13.1
Carriage Services (CSV) 14.7 14.8 10.0
Matthews International (MATW) 27.4 22.3 26.8


Long term, the picture for the company should be positive. But it is important to put all of this in context. For instance, according to one study, the number of deaths per 100,000 in the US has been on the decline. In 1996, 978 people died for every 100,000 people in this country. But by 2024, this number had fallen to 820. If everything goes according to the forecasts, then by the year 2055, this number will fall further to 768. Of course, people will continue to die. In fact, according to the Congressional Budget Office, it's estimated that, without factoring in immigration, the US population will finally see the number of deaths exceed births by the year 2033.

Naturally, we can expect immigration to continue, even in light of recent political events. According to one source, it's believed that, by the year 2050, the total population of the US will be 380.8 million. That compares to the 345.4 million that we saw last year, and it's up materially from the 152.2 million that we saw way back in 1950. Canada should also see its population grow. It should expand from 39.7 million last year to 45.6 million in 2050. So even though the number of deaths per 100,000 continues to drop, the total number of deaths should only rise from here. That will certainly bode well for Service Corporation International.

* in Thousands

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I firmly believe that while Service Corporation International might not be the best prospect on the market, it is an attractive opportunity. Shares are affordable and the industry in which it operates is stable and growing. Over the time that I have been bullish about it, it has outperformed the broader market by a pretty good margin. And in the long run, I fully expect that to continue. Because of these factors, I have no problem keeping the company rated a ‘buy’ for now.

data.title: Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index: Activity Remained Weak In June
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Philly Fed's Manufacturing Business Outlook Survey is a monthly survey of about 250 manufacturers in the Third Federal Reserve District, which covers eastern Pennsylvania, southern New Jersey, and Delaware. Participants of the survey indicate the relative level of general business conditions in the region.

Since this is a diffusion index, negative readings indicate contraction, positive ones indicate expansion. While it focuses exclusively on business in this district, this regional survey gives a reliable direction of the broader Chicago Fed's National Activity Index.

The latest Philadelphia Fed manufacturing index showed weak activity this month. The index was unchanged at -4.0, marking the third straight negative reading. The latest reading was worse than the forecast of -1.7.

Here is the summary from the survey:

Manufacturing activity in the region remained weak, according to the firms responding to the June Manufacturing Business Outlook Survey. The survey’s indicator for current general activity remained slightly negative, unchanged from May. The new orders index fell but remained positive, and the shipments index improved, turning positive; both readings remain below their nonrecession averages. The employment index turned negative and fell to its lowest value since May 2020. Both price indexes moderated but remain elevated. The survey’s future indicators suggest less widespread expectations for growth over the next six months.

The first chart below gives us a look at this diffusion index since 2000, which shows us how it has behaved in proximity to the three 21st century recessions. The green dots show the indicator itself, which is quite noisy. Therefore, we've included the three-month moving average (purple line), which is more useful as an indicator of coincident economic activity.

We can see longer and deeper periods of contraction during each of the recessions with shallower contractions in '02, '03, '11, '12, '13, and '15. Most recently, the index's 3-month moving average contracted from July 2022 to December 2023 however no recession was called during that time.

In the latest report, the 3-month moving average reached its lowest level since November 2023.

In the next chart, we see the complete series, which dates from May 1960. For proof of the high volatility of the headline indicator, note that the average absolute monthly change across this data series is 8.2.

The next chart is an overlay of the General Activity Index and the Future General Activity Index - the outlook six months ahead.

Expectations for Growth Are Less Widespread

The diffusion index for future general activity fell 29 points to 18.3 in June after rising 40 points last month (see Chart 1). The share of firms expecting increases in activity over the next six months (45 percent) exceeded the share expecting decreases (27 percent); 19 percent expect no change. Similarly, the future new orders index dropped 28 points to 22.1, and the shipments index fell 23 points to 27.9. The firms continue to expect overall increases in employment, and the future employment index edged up from 23.0 to 24.6. The future capital expenditures index fell 13 points to 14.5.

For comparison, here is the latest ISM Manufacturing survey.

Let's compare all five Regional Manufacturing indicators. Here is a three-month moving average overlay of each since 2004 (for those with data).

Here is the same chart, including the average of the five. Readers will notice the range in expansion and contraction between all regions.

ETFs associated with industrials and manufacturing include: First Trust Industrials/Producer Durables AlphaDEX Fund (FXR), Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI), Vanguard Industrials ETF (VIS), and iShares U.S. Industrials ETF (IYJ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Performance Food Group: Trouble Brewing Post Acquisitions
data.description:
Business Overview

Performance Food Group (NYSE:PFGC) distributes more than 250,000 food and food-related products across North America. It does so by managing 144 distribution centers to over 300,000 customer locations and employing over 37,000 people. Its customers range from restaurants to schools, businesses, industries, hospitals, retailers, convenience stores, and theaters.

The company's strategy depends heavily on acquisitions. In September 2021, it acquired Core-Mark, expanding its Convenience segment with operations in Canada. In August 2024, the company entered a definitive Stock Purchase Agreement to acquire Cheney Brothers, a transaction valued at over $2 billion. Cheney Brothers will be reported in the Foodservice segment. From the last earnings call transcript:

In the first three quarters of our fiscal year, we have used our balance sheet and cash flow to finance growth initiatives, including two attractive acquisitions, Cheney Brothers and Jose Santiago, and over $300 million in capital projects.

The Foodservice segment consists of food and food-related products distributed to restaurants. These include chain restaurants, family dining, bar and grill, pizza and Italian, and independent restaurants. This segment tends to have lower margins overall but a higher volume. The products distributed include beef, pork, poultry, seafood, frozen foods, refrigerated products, dry groceries, and cleaning and kitchen supplies.

The Vistar segment is the leading national distributor of candy, snacks, beverages, and other items to vending and office coffee services. Retailers, theaters, and hospitals are included as well. Other customers include concessionaires, airports, college bookstores, and correctional facilities. Vending machine products comprise most of the revenue in this segment.

The Convenience segment services mostly the retail industry, with over 50,000 customer locations in the U.S. and Canada. These customers include convenience stores, drug stores, mass merchants, grocery stores, liquor stores, and many others. The products offered include cigarettes, candy, snacks, food, groceries, dairy, bread, beverages, general merchandise, and health & beauty care. There are 35 distribution centers in the U.S. and 4 in Canada.

The company has many suppliers that range from large corporations selling their own products to smaller suppliers operating on a regional basis. The pricing is set either by contract or priced at the time of the order. Contracts are usually based on a percentage markup over cost or a fixed markup per unit. The company typically does not hedge its product prices because the mechanisms of pricing and the fast turnover are usually enough to protect it from rising prices.

Competition is fierce, with numerous national, regional, and local distributors operating across the country. Exclusive agreements for products are very rare, so customers can and often do switch between distributors for marginally better prices. Prices and timely deliveries are usually the most important factors that customers take into consideration.

There is seasonality, with lower profits in the first quarter of each calendar year. Because of the way Performance Food Group reports, this means its third fiscal quarter will often have weaker margins.

Financial Analysis

Performance Food Group has grown 10% in the past two quarters on a YoY basis, mostly because of the Cheney Brothers acquisition.

The M&A muddles the waters. Organic case volume grew 1.3% YoY in the last quarter, which is interesting, but certainly not outstanding. It's also a metric reported by the company, not an audited one.

Gross margins were pretty much in line with the past few years, 11%-12%:

In the call, management expressed concern about potential trouble with tariffs:

Total company cost inflation was about 4.9% for the third quarter, an uptick from our prior period but steady year-to-date. Foodservice product cost inflation was 3.7% in the quarter while Specialty cost inflation was 2.8% year-over-year and Convenience increased by 6.7%. We are closely watching input cost inflation, particularly with the implementation of tariffs, and have not yet seen an impact from tariffs.

EBIT margins were weaker. In particular, this was because of an uptick in SG&A expenses, which might be related to the recent acquisition.

Management didn't address this directly, but I suspect it is caused by the increase in sales headcount:

We have steadily increased our sales force headcount through the year, attracting talent from across the Foodservice landscape. Fiscal year-to-date, our Foodservice sales force headcount increased by 250 associates or 8% year-over-year. This is roughly the pace of hiring we anticipate for the balance of the year, assuming we continue to see positive signs from the consumer.

In another part of the call, management doubles down on this:

So we're living with -- if you look at just from a percentage standpoint when you're growing your people 8% and you're growing your case is 3.4% looking backward, that's going to cost you some money and we're willing to spend that money, and we think we're making the right decision there.

Another expense that increased was the Net Interest Expenses. This is particularly damaging for a low-margin business.

Consequently, the Net Profit Margin was almost halved on a YoY basis. The same happened to Diluted EPS. This is all despite a major acquisition, which should have translated into some form of gain for the shareholders with the increased revenue, but didn't. I expected to see some gains in economies of scale.

The company generates FCF (Cash From Operations minus Cash From Investments) in most quarters when it is not spending money on acquisitions.

The company increased its leverage to pay for the acquisitions, and current Net Debt stands at $7.6B.

Current TTM EBITDA stands at roughly $1.5B, which means the company's leverage is almost 5.00x, which is pretty high in my opinion. It also explains the increase in interest expenses.

Peers, Valuation, Thoughts

These are what I use as PFGC's peers:

PFGC is trading at pretty stretched valuations:

This is surprising to me, given the misses the company has been having:

PFGC's latest diluted EPS was $0.37. The quarter prior to that was $0.27, but let's use the bigger number.

$0.37 * 4 = $1.48

20 * $1.48 = $29.6 (20x current earnings)

30 * $1.48 = $44.4 (30x current earnings)

The stock is currently trading at $86. I don't understand that valuation, and I think there is no margin of safety at all for a BUY case. Not even a HOLD case. I have to go with a SELL here.

The only thing that could change this scenario is if earnings start picking up much faster. Because management is pessimistic about tariffs, and the macro scenario, and insists on increased hiring, I'm not willing to pay upfront for it.

Another important factor will be debt reduction. Management needs to focus on that and slow down acquisitions. Net interest expenses are eating away at profit margins.

Right now, I see this short as asymmetrical. Management needs to work to get earnings back to where they were, but the stock price already reflects that, while the last two quarters show trouble brewing.

As always, thanks for reading, and I wish you a great return this year.

data.title: Northeast Bank: Time For An Upgrade
data.description:
The last few months now have been rather tough for Northeast Bank (NASDAQ:NBN). You see, back in March of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a 'hold' candidate. In the past, I had been bullish about the firm. But after seeing shares skyrocket by 59.8%, I argued that the stock looked more or less fully valued. Since then, things have not gone well. Even though the overall size of the balance sheet continues to expand, and even though recent revenue and profit figures are promising, shares look expense relative to book value and tangible book value.

Admittedly, asset quality is quite high. But up until now, that was not enough to justify a more bullish outlook. After this big drop, however, I do think that the picture has changed. After all, since reaffirming the company as a 'hold' candidate, shares are down 11.8%. That is a world apart from the 4.5% rise that the S&P 500 enjoyed. Because of this return disparity and the continued growth that the company has experienced, I do believe that upgrading it to a soft 'buy' makes sense at this time.

Time to Turn Bullish… Slightly

Since I last wrote about Northeast Bank earlier this year, investors have had new data come out. This data covers the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. At that time, deposits came in at just under $3.30 billion. That compares favorably to the $3.15 billion the bank reported for the second quarter of the year, and it's up quite a bit from the $2.34 billion it ended 2024 with. This growth has not all been good.

You see, a growing portion of the company's overall deposit base is in the form of brokered deposits. These are deposits that are essentially borrowed from other financial institutions. These are not inherently a bad thing, but they are more expensive than traditional deposits are. At the end of the most recent quarter, the company had $1.66 billion of brokered deposits. That compares to the $870.7 million that the firm had at the end of its 2024 fiscal year. This actually represents about 50.4% of its overall deposit base and might be the highest I have seen in this industry.

The good news about the deposit growth is that the firm has been able to grow its loan portfolio as well. The firm went from $2.73 billion in loans at the end of last year to $3.55 billion in the second quarter of 2025. And by the third quarter of this year, they had increased further to $3.69 billion. $2.76 billion of this loan portfolio involves commercial real estate. This is, by a mile, the greatest exposure that the company has. To put this in perspective, in second place, we have commercial and industrial loans at $853 million. Instead of originating its loans, the business gets most of them from other parties. It essentially purchases these loans. In fact, $2.44 billion of its total loans in the most recent quarter were purchased. This compared to $444.4 million that were direct originated loans and $740.8 million that were lender finance loans. Whenever I see a large amount of exposure to commercial loans, which is almost every time I look at a bank, I do worry about exposure to office assets because of rising vacancy rates across the country. The good news is that only $324.1 million, or 8.7%, of the company's loan portfolio involves these types of assets. That's certainly not the lowest that I've seen in the space. But it's not the highest, either.

By comparison, other aspects of the balance sheet are quite small. The value of securities, for instance, is a paltry $28.8 million. Cash is quite a bit higher, but still small, at $344.1 million. But that represents A decline from the $364.9 million that the company had in the second quarter of this year. Meanwhile, debt is modest, totaling $378.5 million in the most recent quarter. While that is up from the $345.2 million that the company ended 2024 with, it does compare favorably to the $407.8 million that the business had at the end of the second quarter of this year.

When it comes to the income statement, we have seen some generally positive results. Even though the company saw its net interest margin contract from the third quarter of 2024 to the third quarter of 2025, dropping from 5.01% to 4.62%, net interest income managed to rise nicely from $35.9 million to $43 million. Things would have been even better had it not been for the fact that the provision for credit losses for the bank actually grew from $0.6 million to $2.9 million. This growth can be attributed to the expanding size of the company's balance sheet. For the first nine months of the 2025 fiscal year, net interest income hit $128.2 million. That compares to the $109.4 million the business reported at the same time one year earlier. Once again, this is in spite of the fact that the firm's net interest margin dropped and the fact that it saw a rise in the provision for credit losses from $1.2 million to $5.3 million. And it can be thanked for the overall growth of the company's balance sheet.

Non-interest income has also been on the rise. But investors shouldn't get terribly excited. Although the firm saw a nice jump from $1.5 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $6.6 million in the third quarter of 2025, that improvement was driven by gains on sales of SBA loans growing from $1 million to $6 million. It would be much better if this growth was because of core operational activities. But that's just not the case here. For the first nine months of 2025, there was a rise from $3.8 million to $16.7 million. But again, gains on sales of SBA loans did the heavy lifting here, expanding from $1.8 million to $14.9 million. These improvements on the top line did allow overall net profits to climb. They grew from $13.9 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $18.7 million in the third quarter of this year. And for the first nine months of this year, the $58.2 million generated by the company beat out the $43.1 million reported one year earlier.

For a company with this kind of consistent growth, and with these high net interest margin readings, we would expect shares to be rather expensive. But relative to earnings, they really aren't. In the chart above, you can see how the company compares to five other similar firms on a price to earnings basis. The 9.3 rating that we get here does make it tied as the cheapest of these six companies shown. And it also places the company within the 6 to 10 range that I like to see. In the chart below, things are a little less attractive. The price to book and price to tangible book multiples put the company around the middle of the group, with three of the five companies I stacked it up against being cheaper than it is.

Asset quality should also be given some attention. And on this front, Northeast Bank really excels. In the first chart below, you can see its return on assets compared to the return on assets of the same five banks I've already stacked it up against. With a reading of 1.86%, Northeast Bank is already meaningfully above the 1% minimum threshold that I like to see. It's also above four of the five companies they compared it to. And in the subsequent chart, you can see the firm's return on equity. At 16.47%, it is leagues above the 10% minimum threshold that I like to see in this space. And it is also greater than four of the five companies I compared it to.

Takeaway

All things considered, I believe that Northeast Bank is an interesting firm. It's unfortunate that shares have fallen so far recently. But honestly, I see this as a buying opportunity. Shares of the company are cheap relative to earnings and asset quality is high. Even though there are some weak spots, overall growth is encouraging. When you add all of this together, I believe that this justifies an upgrade at this time from a 'hold' to a 'buy'.

data.title: AGNC: Rising Mortgage Delinquencies And Agency Privatization Raise Concerns
data.description:
After suffering immense losses in 2022, most mortgage REITs, or mREITs, have been range-bound over the past two years. The iShares Mortgage Real Estate Capped ETF (REM) has traded in a range between $20 and $25 since 2023, currently toward the low end of that range after failing to recover from the April crash. Ever since extreme losses in the 2000s and 2020, investors have been wary of mortgage REITs due to high leverage combined with cyclical risk exposure.

Of course, not all mortgage REITs are the same. I became relatively bearish on agency-backed mortgage REITs around 2021, seeing that higher mortgage rate spreads to Treasuries would cause significant equity erosion once the Federal Reserve stopped purchasing mortgage-backed securities. I last updated my view on AGNC Investment Corp. (NASDAQ:AGNC) in mid-2023, warning of lower cash flows and a dividend cut due to higher borrowing costs. Although it has maintained its high dividend, AGNC's cash flow declined in the middle of last year, while the stock trades at a 9.4% lower price; however, it has delivered a 21% total return due to its exceptional yield.

At the end of last year, I upgraded my outlook on agency-focused mortgage REITs to bullish. In the current environment, I expect well-hedged mortgage REITs to perform well with a potential decline in mortgage rate spreads, low refinancing risk, and generally low systemic mortgage credit risk. Although I've focused on Annaly, most of those factors apply equally, if not more so, to AGNC, given its higher leverage profile.

Goldilocks Environment For Levered Mortgages

The current economic environment is far from ideal for most companies in the real estate industry. Home price growth is slowing as inventories finally rise, with low purchase demand and higher construction activity. This does not bode well for traditional REITs, mortgage originators, and home builders.

However, a frozen property market is OK for mortgage REITs, particularly as it keeps refinancing and prepayment activity low. Although extremely low home affordability likely increases long-term credit risks, the systemic risk is mitigated by the fact that most existing mortgage borrowers borrowed at low rates and low home prices before 2022.

For over a decade, AGNC's book value has hemorrhaged for two primary reasons. One, before 2020, many borrowers paid high-rate mortgages off through refinancing or buying and selling homes at that decade's extremely low mortgage rates. AGNC's portfolio, which is composed almost entirely of fixed-rate residential mortgage-backed securities, devalued as borrowers opted for lower-rate mortgages.

The struggle with refinancing risk ended with higher mortgage rates in 2022. However, by then, the bulk of its portfolio consisted of mortgages at sub-4% or lower rates, which lost significant value as mortgage rates rose. AGNC has some hedge against changes in interest rates through derivatives, but can only hedge Treasury rates, and not the impact of higher mortgage rates relative to Treasuries. This led to significant devaluation, which ultimately resulted in lower operating cash flows. However, it is now in a more stable position, with its book value stagnant and its operating cash flow recovering:

Because AGNC is highly leveraged, with ~$96B in total assets and a $7.9B common tangible book, a TCE of only 8.2%, small interest rate fluctuations have a significant impact on its book value. Its utilization of derivatives on Treasuries limits its exposure to rates in general, but not the spread on mortgage rates compared to Treasuries. This risk factor is detailed in its last 10-Q on the following sensitivity tables:

A considerable 75 bps rise in Treasury rates will only lower AGNC's common book value by about 7%. However, a more minor 50 bps change in MBS spreads will lower AGNC's common book value by a staggering 26%. Its portfolio should only fall 2.6% from such a change, caused by higher rates and lower bond security prices, but exacerbated by AGNC's significant leverage factor. This implies that its common equity faces about 10 times the exposure of its portfolio, as we'd assume given its TCE ratio is close to 10%.

AGNC's Book Value Falling Due to Dilution

Over the past year, the mortgage spread has declined to the benefit of AGNC. The mortgage spread on 30-year rates has dropped to 2%, which is still about 1% above the long-term typical range. See below:

AGNC's book value has risen markedly over the past year due to the decline in mortgage spreads. However, AGNC's book value per share has remained flat due to significant dilution of common equity as it sought to offset lower cash flows with external financing. See below:

When I last covered AGNC, my concern was that it would cut its dividend to offset the quickening decline in operating cash flows. The company avoided dividend cuts by instead selling equity, diluting investors in AGNC. I am not particularly fond of this approach, as there is little benefit to a dividend if a sizable portion of that money is coming from equity dilution. This issue is exacerbated by the fact that, until recently, AGNC was trading at a slight price-to-book discount; hence, its book value per share has declined despite otherwise favorable market conditions.

In my opinion, AGNC, like most mortgage REITs, faces high "agency risk." These terms are unrelated. In this case, AGNC's managers have an incentive to grow their asset portfolios, as that is what drives their income. This encourages the use of excessive leverage and equity dilution through share sales, even when it is seemingly not beneficial to shareholders, given low price-to-book ratios. If it were not for dilution, AGNC's book value per share should have risen over the past year, given the decline in mortgage spreads.

Credit Risk Exposure Is Low, But May Rebound

Unlike banks, mortgage REITs mark their securities at their market value, so even if not realized, interest rate losses or gains are always accounted for in income. Cash flows often differ, but eventually reflect changes in interest rates. Like Anally, AGNC typically incurs losses on devalued securities and repurchases younger mortgages that pay higher rates, enabling it to maintain higher operating cash flows.

On its last 10-Q, AGNC's largest mortgage segment consisted of ~30-year residential mortgage-backed securities with 5.5% fixed interest rates, with a weighted average coupon of 5.05%. This implies most of its holdings were minted since rates rose in 2022, but a decent portion was at lower pre-2022 rates.

AGNC crashed in April as investors braced for an economic recession caused by tariffs. As of June, it is unclear to what extent tariffs will impact the economy, as it is uncertain whether tariffs will remain a key issue. Regardless, considering household debt levels, home price-to-income valuations, home affordability, and low home sales, there are many parallels between today's real estate market and that of twenty years ago, leading up to the mortgage debt crisis; one that nearly ended the mortgage REIT sector.

Both home price-to-income valuations and borrowing costs are higher today than they were at the bubble peak before the 2007 crash. Those characteristics of a bubble are objectively worse today than they were then. However, a smaller portion of mortgage borrowers fall into this "high risk" category, while loan-to-value ratios are strong. Still, affordability is terribly low, and mortgage delinquencies are now rising as a result:

Because AGNC's portfolio has a weighted average yield of around 5%, a sizable portion of those mortgages are likely in loans made at extremely low affordability after 2021. It is virtually inevitable that loans made at 5%+ rates and record-high home price-to-income ratios will have higher delinquency levels than mortgages created before 2021 at lower home prices and rates.

The system risk is not high. Mortgage debt service payments as a percent of disposable income are about 6% today, in line with typical levels before the 2000s housing bubble became extreme around 2005. Still, AGNC's portfolio will likely face higher delinquencies because it's focused on younger mortgages means it has greater exposure to those with higher debt service costs.

I disagree with the view that AGNC has no credit risk because its assets are backed by Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) and Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC). The fact is that those two companies remain poorly capitalized and cannot withstand a huge systemic increase in mortgage defaults. This risk will be exacerbated if Trump manages to re-privatize them, although the government is under no obligation to bail out the agencies even if they remain government-owned.

Even the remote risk of financial stress for the agencies could be enough to devalue MBS securities sufficiently for AGNC to face losses. Even if the agencies should be able to manage delinquencies, widespread concern about this risk has caused MBS to decline sharply during most equity market crashes. Hence, AGNC suffered significant losses in April 2020, despite neither having a notable adverse effect on mortgage fundamentals. Of course, given that delinquencies are rising, we cannot be certain that AGNC's portfolio is recession-proof. Ultimately, the agency guarantee is only as good as the agencies, which are sufficiently capitalized for a small risk event but likely not for a repeat of the massive mortgage delinquencies seen after 2007.

The Bottom Line

If we assume no huge increase in mortgage delinquencies and a shift away from dilutive shareholder practices, I would be bullish on AGNC. For the first time since the 2000s, AGNC does not face high refinancing risks, given it is unlikely that mortgage rates will return to 3% anytime soon. AGNC also does not face high interest rate risks, given that mortgage spreads are still historically elevated and therefore are unlikely to rise. Mortgage rates could rise as inflation rebounds, but I expect that would be mirrored by an increase in Treasury rates, which AGNC is hedged against.

AGNC's price-to-book is now around 1.12, so equity sales would not have the same dilutive impact as they had last year. Still, because AGNC sold equity while its price-to-book was so low last year, I am cautious toward its shareholder value stance. Further, although I do not expect a repeat of the 2008 scenario, the combination of higher mortgage delinquencies and privatization of the GSEs certainly raises AGNC's risk exposure.

Overall, I am neutral on AGNC. I expect it will remain stable and continue to pay an extraordinary dividend yield; however, it faces a risk of sharp declines, particularly if delinquencies accelerate significantly during a recession.

data.title: Protagonist Therapeutics: Two 'Slam Dunk' Sets Of Pivotal Data Set Up Bull Case
data.description:
Investment Overview

I have covered Protagonist Therapeutics (NASDAQ:PTGX) twice before in notes for Seeking Alpha, in March 2021, issuing a "Buy" recommendation, and in February last year, issuing a "Hold" call.

Last February I explained how Protagonist stock had nose-dived in September 2021, after a clinical hold was placed on its lead candidate rusfertide, due to preclinical data showing evidence of skin tumors in a mouse model study, recovered after the clinical hold was lifted, then nose-dived again in April 2022 as the FDA attempted to rescind the Breakthrough Designation it had awarded to rusfertide.

Shares slipped <$10 in late 2022 - at the time of my 2021 note, they traded >$30 - but then came roaring back as Protagonist's partner, the Pharma giant Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), shared promising data from its second lead candidate, JNJ-2113, now known as icotrokinra, in a Phase 2 study of patients with psoriasis.

Protagonist also agreed a licensing deal for rusfertide - a hepcidin mimetic peptide of the natural hormone hepcidin, indicated for the rare blood disorder polycythemia vera ("PV") - last February. Protagonist received an upfront payment of $300m, plus the promise of regulatory milestone payments, as well as commercial milestones and tiered royalties on ex-U.S. net sales

Given the stock is now up 90% since my "Buy" call last February (I wish I had maintained a "Buy" rating), I wanted to update the investment thesis and rule over whether a rating upgrade may still be applied.

Updates Since My Last Note - JNJ Plans NDA For Oral Icotrokinra

After my February 2024 note, shares kept climbing - from $28, to a >$45 per share by November, when Johnson & Johnson released positive Phase 3 data from its ICONIC-LEAD Phase 3 study of icotrokinra in patients with moderate to severe plaque psoriasis.

JNJ reported that:

At week 16, nearly two-thirds (64.7%) of patients treated with icotrokinra achieved IGA scores of 0/1 (clear or almost clear skin), and 49.6% achieved PASI 90, compared to 8.3% and 4.4% on placebo, respectively.

Further increases in response rates continued to be observed at week 24, with 74.1% of patients treated with icotrokinra achieving IGA scores of 0/1, and 64.9% achieving PASI 90.

A similar proportion of patients experienced adverse events (AES) between icotrokinra and placebo, with 49.3% and 49.1% of participants experiencing a treatment emergent adverse event (TEAE) at week 16.

Icotrokinra is a once-daily, orally taken IL-23 antagonist. That's a similar mechanism of action to JNJ's drug Stelara (ustekinumab), a monoclonal antibody targeting IL-12 and IL-23, which earned >$10bn revenues in 2024 across indications including psoriasis, Crohn's Disease and ulcerative colitis.

Stelara's patent protection is set to expire next year, however, and JNJ hopes that icotrokinra's oral availability will give it a competitive edge over rivals such as AbbVie's (ABBV) Skyrizi, for example, and help off-set lost Stelara revenues. Analysts and JNJ management have speculated the drug could earn >$5bn in peak annual revenues.

From Protagonist's perspective, in relation to icotrokinra, the company expects to earn, before 2028, $50m upon receipt of any marketing approval in any indication, $25m on acceptance of a new drug application, and $45m on receipt of a marketing approval in a second indication.

Furthermore, Protagonist says there is $630m of future milestones available and royalties on net sales of 6-10%. In March this year, Protagonist stock surged to an all-time high of $55 per share, as JNJ released final ICONIC-LEAD results, showing "nearly two-thirds (65%) of patients" achieved clear or almost clear skin, and 50% achieved a PASI 90 response, i.e. a 90% or greater improvement in the Psoriasis Area and Severity Index (PASI) score.

JNJ plans to submit for approval of icotrokina and commented on its Q1 earnings call that:

we really believe that icotrokinra is a big market expansion opportunity to get in those earlier lines of therapy and to bring patients into therapies that have that strong biologic-like efficacy.

The company will push for label expansions into parallel indications and is already running head-to-head studies, in the hope of demonstrating its oral pill's superiority over injectables.

In summary, then, icotrokinra appears to be a "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) in waiting, however Protagonist's share of any success is relatively modest, and we may note Protagonist's current market cap of $3.34bn. In the most optimistic scenario, Protagonist is unlikely to earn >$500m per annum from this drug, albeit it does not have to pay sales and marketing costs.

Updates Since My Last Note - Takeda Co-Commercialization Of Rusfertide

As regarding the Takeda deal and Rusfertide, the situation is more nuanced.

As we can see above (slide from a recent Protagonist presentation) Protagonist can either "opt-in" to co-commercialization, and earn $630m in milestones, royalties on overseas sales and a 50:50 share of profits in the U.S.< or "opt out", and earn up $1.675bn in milestones, higher overseas royalties, but no share of U.S. revenues.

According to Protagonist's Q1 2025 quarterly report / 10-Q submission, in relation to its Phase 3 VERIFY clinical study of rusfertide:

In March 2025, we announced positive top-line data for the trial’s 32-week primary efficacy endpoint, potentially leading to an NDA filing in the fourth quarter of 2025.

The primary endpoint of the study was met, with a significantly higher proportion of clinical responders among rusfertide-treated patients with PV (77%) compared to those who received the placebo (33%) during weeks 20-32; p<0.0001. The primary endpoint of the study was the proportion of patients achieving a response, which was defined as the absence of phlebotomy eligibility.

A phlebotomy is a blood-letting procedure carried out at a hospital or a health clinic, and the current standard of care in PV. Rusfertide, a self-injectable therapy, restricts overproduction of red blood cells, keeping hematocrit (the volume percentage of red blood cells in blood) levels lower and keeping patients out of the hospital.

The Phase 3 study met all primary and secondary endpoints, seemingly making approval a formality, and opening up a $1bn - $2bn peak annual revenue opportunity, Protagonist believes, based on 80k patients receiving PV treatments today.

That gives Protagonist a tricky decision to make, whether to play a more passive or a more active role in the commercial rollout of the drug. Financing is not really a problem for the company, which reported net income of $275m last year as a result of the Takeda deal, and, at the end of last quarter, $574m of cash and equivalents.

Analysis - Two 'Slam Dunks' Plus A Shot At Entering Obesity Markets Merits Rating An Upgrade

I would not say Protagonist stock is exceptionally cheap relative to the opportunity in front of the company, being up 230% on a five-year basis, and >65% on a 12-months basis, but the company has delivered two "slam dunk" sets of data in the past year, for two products that I'd speculate will earn billions of dollars of revenues going forward.

The revenues Protagonist could earn by 2030, let's say, if both drugs are approved could be anything from $500m - $1bn, based on milestones plus royalties, whatever decision Protagonist makes in relation to the Takeda agreement.

What adds to the value of the company however in my view is how successful its peptide programs have been, which gives it the ability to make deals with other large Pharma's potentially, or become an M&A target, and in that scenario a Pharma could pay a double-digit billion sum, potentially, or let's say >$7.5bn for access to the deals, royalties, milestones, commercial revenues, and pipeline prospects.

One particular pipeline program excites me. Although preclinical, Protagonist hopes to enter the anti-obesity space, and announce development candidates this year.

While both Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) - whose injectable anti-obesity / diabetes drugs referred to in the slide above, tirzepatide and semaglutide, earned >$40bn of revenues between them last year - have promised oral versions of their drug will be on the market soon, a successful weight loss program could add billions to the market cap of Protagonist, and make it even more of an acquisition target, such is the hype around oral incretin mimetic peptides.

Given I was cautious about Protagonist's ability to deliver in the clinic last year, and missed out on >90% upside, on this occasion, I am upgrading my rating to a "Buy." The company now has an established track record and its shares look a decent bet for an investor looking for steady, or sudden, upside, based on revenue potential, upcoming approval catalysts, data catalysts (for icotrokinra), and new programs entering the clinic, such as oral peptides indicated for obesity.

data.title: Freegold Ventures Is Well-Positioned Amid Promising Drilling In Alaska And Bullish Gold
data.description:
A Buy Rating for Freegold Ventures Limited

Thanks to a strong positive correlation with the price of gold, the US shares of Freegold Ventures Limited (OTCQX:FGOVF), a Vancouver, Canada-based company exploring precious and base metals, have so far benefited noticeably from the rapid rise in gold prices. The bullish trend is supported by drilling results that reveal very promising prospects for increasing gold resources at the flagship Golden Summit project, just a 30-minute drive from Fairbanks. This project is gaining momentum in light of the record price, and this interest is driving Freegold Ventures Limited shares ever higher, with a growth rate that is enviable even among much more reputable and established companies in the global gold exploration and mining industry.

Freegold Ventures Limited’s Performance Compared to the Global Industry

Thanks to this price rally, Freegold Ventures Limited, listed on the US OTCQX market, gained 147.25%, outperforming the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX)’s plus-59.11% over the past 12 months. The VanEck Gold Miners ETF Index is the benchmark for the performance of publicly traded gold exploration and mining companies, globally operating.

At the bottom of the TradingView chart, there is a yellow area curve representing a positive correlation between the company and the gold price. This relationship means that when gold prices rise, FGOVF's share price is likely to be in the uptrend mode, and when gold prices fall, FGOVF's share price is probably moving downwards. This is the essence of the positive correlation. This information is useful to know because the forecasts by the analysts (as we illustrate later) continue to push the gold price higher, encouraged by a favorable environment for shares of Freegold to keep on prospering. As drilling at the Golden Summit project picks up pace and reveals expandable and upgradeable gold ore deposits, FGOVF shares are expected to extend the previous period of growth.

The Gold Price Outlook Plus Forecasts

Let's look at the most common gold price benchmark.

The Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) is $3,376.71 per ounce, representing an increase of 26.73% year-to-date and 44.61% over the past 12 months, at the time of this writing. This increase is solely due to demand for safe-haven investments, as the catalyst of hopes of an interest rate cut is currently not in operation.

The upside in the price of gold is primarily driven by demand for safe-haven assets, as investors seize the opportunity for appreciation of the troy ounce during times when macroeconomic uncertainty and geopolitical tensions threaten the value of their portfolios.

Hopes for lower interest rates in the US also typically contribute to a positive sentiment towards gold, as lower interest rates dampen demand for US Treasury bonds, which in turn benefits gold, which, in contrast, does not yield interest. This positive catalyst is currently missing, as the US Federal Reserve left interest rates unchanged at 4.25%-4.50% in June 2025 for the fourth consecutive month. This is because US Fed policymakers prefer to play it safe, as they cannot assess the impact of tariffs on the US economic outlook. We suspect US Fed policymakers don't want to make the mistake of underestimating the tariffs, as Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell said:

"What we've learned is that ... tariffs are going to be substantially larger than forecasters generally thought,"

AsYahoo Finance reported in its article “Fed Chair Powell: 'Substantially larger' tariffs are raising inflation expectations” on Wednesday, June 18, 2025, at 9:51 PM GMT+2.

The market narrative now suggests that the Fed will cut the federal funds rate twice, each by 25 basis points, before the end of 2025. CME FedWatch, which tracks the probabilities of Fed interest rate changes based on 30-day fed funds futures prices, estimates the probability of a 25 basis point cut to a range of 400–425 at 57.3% at the time of writing, down from 59.4% a week ago. CME FedWatch forecasts a 42.1% probability of a further 25 basis point cut to the 375-400 range, slightly higher than the 40.2% forecast a week ago, at the time of this writing.

On Wall Street, it's not all interest rate cuts; it appears that analysts don't expect any interest rate cuts this year. This is weighing on the Fed's interest rate optimism, which, as mentioned, already appears less enthusiastic than before the Fed's unchanged interest rate decision on June 18. This market sentiment regarding interest rates, which revolves around the Fed's continued “wait-and-see” stance, probably means that the gold price is currently not really benefiting from expectations of lower interest rates.

The Gold Price Forecasts

Regarding the gold price forecasts as published in the Equity Insider News Commentary on May 27, 2025, 9:50 AM ET, on behalf of RUA GOLD Inc. (RUA:CA) via PRNewswire and reported by Seeking Alpha:

Safe-haven demand, rate cut expectations, and fresh volatility tied to US President Donald Trump's tariff warnings have sent bullion prices soaring, prompting Citi to raise its short-term gold target to $3,500. Bank of America followed by increasing its gold exposure in Q4, citing a bullish shift in underlying market conditions.

[…]

Veteran investor Rob McEwen and billionaire John Paulson both see $5,000 gold within reach, while JPMorgan recently projected a potential path to $6,000 if even a sliver of U.S.-held foreign assets are reallocated toward bullion.

About Freegold Ventures Limited's Gold Mineral Interest In Alaska

In its report “Freegold Provides Update on 2025 Drill Program”, released on May 29, 2025, at 5:00 AM ET and published by Seeking Alpha, the company describes itself as follows:

Freegold is a TSX-listed company focused on exploration in Alaska. It holds the Golden Summit Gold Project near Fairbanks and the Shorty Creek Copper-Gold Project near Livengood through leases.

Freegold Ventures Limited's portfolio of gold mineral resources is well-positioned under US President Donald Trump, as his Administration 2.0 promotes the exploitation of domestic mineral resources.

As Seeking Alpha reported on March 24, 2025, at 12:52 PM ET, citing another company, on March 20, 2025,

President Trump signed an executive order calling for increased U.S. mineral production,

In our view, this order also improves the prospects for the Alaska projects of Freegold Ventures Limited, particularly the Golden Summit project, which appears to be more advanced than Shorty Creek.

In the just mentioned report, Seeking Alpha added:

The order focuses on securing a stable supply of essential minerals such as copper and gold for defense, technology, and infrastructure, while directing federal agencies to expedite domestic mineral project approvals.

President Trump's Executive Order is giving Freegold Ventures Limited shares a positive boost because the state of Alaska, where Golden Summit and Shorty Creek are located, is both a mining-friendly area and has abundant natural resources. These resources could become a huge reservoir of wealth for the U.S. economy through the mining activities of operators like Freegold Ventures.

Sprott’s Mining Risk Map shows that Alaska, like North America, has a very mining and exploration-friendly jurisdiction and environment, resulting in very little, if any, investment risk. Sprott is a global asset manager with decades of experience in physical investments in metals and minerals, as well as close relationships and expertise in the world of the mining industry.

The reduced or even non-existent investment risk is primarily reflected in the lower financing costs for developing, operating and expanding metal production resources. This future scenario of falling borrowing costs could be unattractive for lenders looking to raise capital at the most favorable terms. The US central bank may take a more accommodative stance in the future than it does now, suggesting a shift in the signal that could encourage Freegold to borrow and accelerate its gold projects.

The current stance of US policymakers continues to lead to excessively high borrowing costs and suggests a continued heightened risk of default for businesses and households struggling to make ends meet near the end of the month. Instead, with interest rates expected to cool from September onwards, Freegold's prospects for success in Alaska may gradually improve, opening the door to substantial external financing – capital requests that lenders are unlikely to turn down for three reasons:

A) because of President Trump’s executive orders to develop the United States’ domestic mineral resources and reduce the current over-reliance on foreign resources.

B) because conditions must be restored to stimulate economic growth in the United States, which, according to the US GDP, contracted for the first time in three years in the first quarter of 2025. This is, according to Trading Economics, the status of the GDP in the US at the time of this writing, based on sources from the US Bureau of Economic Analysis.

C) because, with gold prices perching at record-high levels, borrowing liquidity to finance these projects is inherently a profitable investment for lenders, even if the market imposes significantly lower interest rates. A higher gold price in the future is consistent with a vision that foresees an increasing demand for gold bullion as a safe asset, coinciding with a likely turbulent, threatening and tense global environment.

The State of Alaska Is Rich In Natural Resources

Alaska is undoubtedly rich in natural resources, and its impressive economic power can be extremely beneficial to the American economy and Alaskan society, as evidenced by some of the statistics we present here. Freegold Ventures is well positioned in this context, as it could be one of the players involved in the case to dig up the stored wealth.

Alaska is the second most gold-producing state in the U.S., says the Bureau of Land Management of Alaska.

The U.S. Geological Survey Mineral Commodity Summaries 2024 report shows that gold, primarily but also copper and other metals, together, as Alaska's production of non-hydrocarbon minerals is the sixth largest in the United States in U.S. dollar terms.

Golden Summit Gold Project Near Fairbanks

The Golden Summit Project is located 28 km northeast of Fairbanks, Alaska. Mineral resources are estimated to be primarily derived from the Dolphin/Cleary resource, as the Dolphin area is largely unexplored.

The Dolphin-Cleary gold deposit contains 13.106 million ounces of gold in indicated mineral resources at an average grade of around 1.04 grams per tonne of ore and 11.339 million ounces of gold in inferred mineral resources at an average grade of around 1.07 grams per tonne of ore, based on a September 2024 estimate, with a further update to be completed before June 30, 2025.

Golden Summit was scheduled to resume drilling after its winter break in mid-May, marking the start of a comprehensive 2025 drilling program. Based on the drilling results, particularly from the WOW Zone, a resource expansion target extending 1,500 meters west of the currently defined Dolphin Cleary deposit, the company expects both growth and improvement in resource grade. In one of its drilling reports dated April 23, 2025, Freegold Ventures Limited informed the market about the 2025 drilling campaign and wrote:

[…] This program will commence with two drill rigs and increase to four rigs by early summer, with a total of approximately 30,000 meters planned. All four rigs are currently on site. The program aims to be comprehensive, focusing on converting inferred mineral resources into the indicated category and drilling additional metallurgical holes in preparation for a planned Pre-Feasibility Study (PFS).

A preliminary feasibility study will be of great importance and Freegold Ventures shares could receive a boost upon its release, as it will provide not only technical but also some economic details on the development of a mine at Golden Summit.

The presence of Kinross Gold Corporation's (KGC) (K:CA) Fort Knox Mine just 6 km to the south certainly bodes well for the future construction of a prolific gold mine in the Golden Summit project area, centered on the Dolphin/Cleary resources, as the Fort Knox Mine has produced over 9 million ounces to date. The Dolphin/Cleary gold resource basin has a mineralization system similar in age to Kinross Gold's Fort Knox.

In its first quarter 2025 results, Kinross Gold reports that gold equivalent production at Fort Knox (approximately 22% of the total 512,088 gold equivalent ounces produced by the company in the first quarter of 2025) is increasing (+110% year-on-year and +7% quarter-on-quarter), supported by the commencement of Manh Choh production in the second half of 2024. This confirms the expandability of the gold resources in this district. In addition, costs continue to decline: Fort Knox's production costs per ounce sold reached $1,176 in the first quarter of 2025 compared to $1,466 in the first quarter of 2024. Total production costs per ounce sold in the US segment were $1,240 in the first quarter of 2025 compared to $1,312 in the first quarter of 2024. The company's total attributable production costs were $1,038 per ounce in the first quarter of 2025 compared to $982 per ounce in the first quarter of 2024.

Inferred or indicated mineral resources at Dolphin/Cleary deposits are not economically provable. Therefore, they must first pass the assessment to be included in the higher category of measured mineral reserves and ultimately in the highest category of proven or at least probable mineral reserves. Only then will it become clear how much the Dolphin/Cleary gold mine could produce and at what rates of return and cost.

About 125 kilometres by road northwest of Fairbanks lies Freegold Ventures' other project, called "Shorty Creek", a copper-gold project, which, however, is still at a very early stage compared to Golden Summit.

Raising Capital To Finance Mineral Resource Updates: An Indication of the Intrinsic Value of Freegold Ventures Limited Shares

Freegold finances exploration and related activities with capital from the issuance of common shares or the potential conversion of warrants into common shares. On April 03, the company closed the agreement with Paradigm Capital for a proposed private placement, which is expected to raise up to ≈ C$42 million (≈US$30.6 million as of this writing). The C$42 million will be raised through an offering of 49,383,300 units, each valued at C$0.85 price. This offering provides a rough estimate of the intrinsic value of one common share of Freegold Ventures, as each unit entitles the holder to one common share and one-half warrant to acquire one common share. Investors should note that the parties, including the financier, Paradigm Capital, have agreed to an exercise price of C$1.30 per warrant share, exercisable for a period of 24 months from the closing date. Since math is not an opinion, the growth potential is substantial and is backed by the continued whipsaw soar in the gold price and the development of gold mineral resources through drilling. The Golden Summit gold project is valuable, and the goal of the drillers is to establish an estimate of the ounces that can be profitably mined in the future.

Cash Burn and Cash Runaway

The company must cover general and administrative expenses of ≈ US$350,000 and exploration and evaluation costs of ≈ US$765,000 (as of Q1 2025). The latter cost category is capitalized and has so far resulted in a balance sheet total assets of US$107.3 million as of the first quarter of 2025.
